Name: adaptive_layer_skipping
Title: Adaptive Layer Skipping for Latency-Aware Inference in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: Implementing a structured adaptive layer-skipping mechanism based on Jacobian evaluations can significantly reduce inference latency without compromising accuracy, thereby addressing the needs of latency-critical applications.
Related Work: Existing literature focuses on layer skipping or network pruning but often lacks a systematic approach to adaptively skip layers in real-time based on the input characteristics. This proposal uniquely combines Jacobian evaluations with a PAC-Bayes framework to bound overhead and optimize inference latency.
Abstract: In latency-critical applications, optimizing inference time while maintaining accuracy is paramount. This proposal introduces an adaptive layer-skipping mechanism informed by Jacobian evaluations, allowing for dynamic layer selection based on input features. By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks, we will derive strict latency bounds while minimizing hidden-state perturbation. Our approach is designed to be compute-frugal, utilizing single-pass adaptive thresholds to ensure memory efficiency. We will conduct experiments to evaluate throughput-accuracy trade-offs, benchmarking against existing methods while accounting for memory bandwidth and perturbation costs. This research aims to push the boundaries of latency-aware inference in neural networks, providing a robust framework for future applications in real-time systems.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline neural network model and modify it to include the adaptive layer-skipping mechanism.
2. Conduct experiments with varying input conditions to evaluate the performance of layer skipping based on Jacobian evaluations, using metrics such as inference time, accuracy, and memory usage.
3. Compare the proposed method against standard layer skipping and traditional inference methods to measure throughput-accuracy trade-offs.
4. Analyze perturbation costs and quantify stability radii to ensure compliance with latency budgets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The adaptive mechanism might introduce overhead that negates latency benefits in certain scenarios.
2. The reliance on Jacobian evaluations may complicate the implementation in highly dynamic environments.
3. Results may vary significantly across different neural network architectures, limiting generalizability.
